interactions.summary: Jiang explains the school mechanics: he records an actual class, uploads it, and teaches private-school Chinese students preparing for study abroad in English-speaking Western institutions.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang answer to Kim's school questions.

interactions.summary: Kim presses how Jiang can teach conspiracy theories and raw material in China; Jiang answers autobiographically that Yale-style elite education failed to explain Trump, COVID, and Ukraine, so he began testing conspiracy frameworks as ways to connect dots and teach an attitude rather than facts.

speaker_notes.note: SPEAKER_00 remains Jiang; SPEAKER_01 remains Kim. Some ASR phrases in seg-0019 and seg-0024 are damaged but the argument structure is clear.

claims.claim: Jiang's class is not a public-school Gaokao track; it is a private school for Chinese students intending to study abroad, with English instruction and Western history and philosophy as preparation.
Jiang says his elite Yale education failed him because it could not prepare him to understand Trump's 2016 victory, COVID, or the Ukraine war.
He says conspiracy frameworks became worth testing because they sometimes offered a more coherent picture of the world than elite education's disconnected facts.
claims.temporal_scope: Method turn described as emerging during the COVID-era years before 2026-04-01.

claims.claim: Jiang says he teaches students not facts but an attitude: question things, connect dots, and risk uncomfortable truths.

signature_moments.moment: The elite education that should have explained the world becomes the thing that blinds him to it.
signature_moments.source_phrase: this elite education has failed me
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives Jiang's conspiracy turn an autobiographical engine rather than treating it as mere provocation.

signature_moments.moment: Teaching becomes an attitude of connecting dots even when the trail leads to uncomfortable truths.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the education thesis behind the whole channel and later public read.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's phrase for replacing disconnected elite facts with a framework that links events into causal patterns.
The Yale-style formation Jiang says gave credentials and blind spots but did not explain recent political crises.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang dates his turn toward conspiracy frameworks to the period after 2016 and especially the COVID years, when he says he tried to understand Trump, COVID, Ukraine, 9/11, the moon landing, and JFK through alternative frameworks.

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR phrase 'bar's colleges, such as Carlton' likely refers to U.S. liberal arts colleges such as Carleton, but the packet preserves the grounded meaning without normalizing the name.
ASR renders COVID as 'Colbert' near the list of topics; surrounding context supports COVID as the intended referent.
